title: Web Application Usability and Legal Issues
breadcrumbs: Web Application Usability and Legal Issues
text: Web Application Usability and Legal Issues There are a number of important issues that you should address when creating a Web site. Consider doing the following to minimize the risk of legal action: Clearly separate and label different services and functions of your Web site to prevent confusion. If you use common business metaphors (such as a storefront or daily newspaper) reproduce the "look and feel" of the metaphor as closely as possible. Do not mix conflicting attributes or different metaphors. If your Web site simply transmits an information stream originating outside your site, do not interfere with the transmitted content without good reason. If you republish material originating from outside your site, ensure that you have the necessary permissions. If you republish material from a public source, ensure that it is clearly in the public domain. Have visitors to your site agree to a contract. This can greatly reduce their legal claims against you. In particular, make them agree that they use the information on your site at their own risk, and that they are responsible to you, if they put something on your site that injures someone else. Identify visitors to your Web site. This can greatly reduce your liability for their actions. Put reasonable site management policies in place for responding to problem materials that can appear at your Web site. Ensure that all your advertising and publicity material has been carefully reviewed so as not to raise the legal standards applied to your Web site. Do not have links to Web sites that you know or strongly suspect carry illegal or dangerous materials. Use disclaimers for services that pose special legal risks, or which are subject to government regulation. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
